refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-003 Refined: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and tele-operators to achieve a measured energy efficiency of energy_per_ton ≤ E_target (customer-defined threshold) over the agreed evaluation period, through integrated monitoring, control optimization, and real-time feedback mechanisms.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is outcome-focused but lacks explicit linkage to the mechanisms (monitoring, control, feedback) that enable efficiency achievement. Refinement clarifies that energy efficiency is not a passive property but an active capability requiring measurement, optimization logic, and operator awareness. This supports both direct operation and teleoperation modes.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure energy_per_ton = total_energy_consumed / total_tonnage_moved over a defined evaluation period (e.g., 40-hour work week). Verify that measured value ≤ E_target. Collect telemetry from Vehicle power systems and cross-reference with load/distance data. Conduct controlled trials under representative worksite conditions with both on-site and tele-operated modes.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and log energy consumption (kWh or equivalent) from all propulsion and auxiliary systems.
CR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and log tonnage moved (mass × distance or equivalent work metric).
CR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time energy efficiency feedback (energy_per_ton) to operators and tele-operators via Telemetry interface.
CR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall support operator/tele-operator control strategies that minimize energy consumption while meeting worksite tasking requirements.
CR-NGHE-003.5: The NGHE solution shall log and report energy efficiency KPI data for post-evaluation analysis and continuous improvement.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement energy monitoring and efficiency calculation logic within the Vehicle control system; provide real-time KPI feedback via Telemetry to operators; enable operator-driven efficiency optimization through control parameter tuning.

DP-B: Implement a centralized Energy Management Service (separate from Vehicle) that aggregates telemetry from Vehicle, calculates efficiency KPIs, and pushes feedback via Telemetry; Vehicle remains responsible for power measurement only.

DP-C: Implement energy efficiency monitoring and optimization across both Vehicle (power measurement) and Teleoperation (remote control strategy optimization); coordinate via Tasking and Telemetry interfaces to enable tele-operator-driven efficiency improvements.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Implement energy efficiency monitoring and optimization across both Vehicle (power measurement and real-time efficiency calculation) and Teleoperation (remote control strategy optimization and feedback presentation). Vehicle measures energy consumption and tonnage, calculates energy_per_ton, and transmits via Telemetry. Teleoperation receives Telemetry, presents efficiency feedback to tele-operators, and optimizes control commands (via Tasking) to minimize energy per unit work. This design supports both on-site and remote operation modes and distributes responsibility according to domain expertise.

potential_sources: Vehicle and Teleoperation must synchronize on energy_per_ton calculation methodology to avoid inconsistent KPI reporting.
Telemetry interface must carry sufficient granularity (instantaneous power, cumulative energy, tonnage, efficiency ratio) to enable real-time feedback and optimization.
Tasking interface must support efficiency-aware control commands (e.g., speed profiles, load distribution) that Teleoperation can issue based on efficiency feedback.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C exhibits functional coupling between Vehicle measurement and Teleoperation optimization, but this is intentional and manageable via well-defined interfaces (Telemetry for data flow, Tasking for control). The coupling is not circular and supports the dual-mode (on-site + remote) operational requirement. Decoupling further (DP-B) would reduce Vehicle responsibility but add a third module; DP-C balances simplicity with capability.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Vehicle power measurement systems are accurate and calibrated to ±5% or better.
Assumption: Tonnage measurement (via load cells, GPS, or equivalent) is available and integrated into Vehicle telemetry.
Assumption: Tele-operators have sufficient training and situational awareness to interpret efficiency feedback and adjust control strategies.
Risk: If Vehicle and Teleoperation use different efficiency calculation methods, KPI values may diverge, causing confusion and audit failures.
Risk: Real-time efficiency feedback may overwhelm operators if not presented clearly; poor UI/UX could negate optimization benefits.
Risk: Network latency in Teleoperation mode may delay efficiency feedback, reducing tele-operator responsiveness to efficiency opportunities.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify the energy_per_ton calculation formula and ownership: Vehicle shall compute and transmit raw energy and tonnage data; Teleoperation (or a shared service) shall compute and present energy_per_ton KPI. This avoids dual calculation and ensures single source of truth. Additionally, specify Telemetry data rate and latency requirements to support real-time feedback in tele-operation mode.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: The Vehicle module owns energy consumption measurement, tonnage tracking, real-time efficiency calculation (energy_per_ton), and transmission of efficiency telemetry. It also receives and executes efficiency-aware control commands from Teleoperation via the Tasking interface. The Vehicle module includes propulsion systems, auxiliary systems, power measurement hardware/firmware, load measurement sensors, and embedded control logic.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle transmits real-time energy consumption (kWh), tonnage moved (tons), calculated energy_per_ton (kWh/ton), and efficiency status flags to Teleoperation and Safety Services. Data rate: ≥1 Hz for real-time feedback; latency: <500 ms for tele-operation responsiveness.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives control commands from Teleoperation (and on-site operator interface) that may include efficiency-aware parameters (e.g., eco-mode, speed profile, load distribution hints). Vehicle interprets and executes these commands within safety and operational constraints.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives safety-critical alerts from Safety Services (e.g., energy system fault, overload condition) and may adjust efficiency optimization to prioritize safety.

module_containment_scores.notes: Vehicle is the natural owner of energy measurement, power system control, and real-time efficiency calculation. All hardware sensors (power, load) and embedded firmware reside here. Diffusion risk is minimal: only the presentation and optimization strategy logic (owned by Teleoperation) is external. High containment is appropriate.

Teleoperation could own efficiency feedback presentation and tele-operator-driven optimization strategy, but would require Vehicle to transmit raw data and Teleoperation to compute KPI. This splits measurement and calculation, increasing diffusion. Teleoperation is a secondary owner for optimization logic only.

Safety Services could monitor energy efficiency as a safety indicator (e.g., anomalous energy consumption as a fault signal), but primary responsibility for efficiency KPI belongs to Vehicle and Teleoperation. Safety Services is a consumer of efficiency telemetry, not an owner.

NGHE Solution (system-level) could own the overall energy efficiency capability, but this is too abstract. Responsibility must be allocated to concrete modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation). NGHE Solution integrates the capability but does not implement it.

assigned_im.ownership_candidate: Vehicle (primary), with Teleoperation as secondary owner for optimization strategy and feedback presentation.
assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Energy efficiency measurement and real-time KPI calculation are best contained within the Vehicle module because (1) all energy measurement hardware and sensors reside in Vehicle, (2) real-time calculation requires low-latency access to power and load data, (3) Vehicle firmware can optimize control strategies based on efficiency feedback without external round-trips. Teleoperation owns the presentation layer and tele-operator-driven optimization strategy, which is a logical separation of concerns.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle firmware becomes too complex (measurement + calculation + optimization), it may exceed embedded system capacity or introduce bugs. Mitigation: Keep Vehicle firmware focused on measurement and basic calculation; delegate advanced optimization to Teleoperation.

Risk: If Telemetry interface does not carry sufficient data granularity, Teleoperation cannot perform independent efficiency analysis or audit. Mitigation: Specify Telemetry to include raw energy and tonnage data, not just computed KPI.

Risk: If Vehicle and Teleoperation use different efficiency calculation methods, KPI values diverge. Mitigation: Define a single, canonical energy_per_ton formula and assign calculation ownership (recommend Vehicle for real-time, Teleoperation for post-analysis).
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify the split of responsibility: Vehicle shall measure energy and tonnage, compute energy_per_ton in real-time, and transmit via Telemetry. Teleoperation shall receive Telemetry, present efficiency feedback to tele-operators, and issue efficiency-aware control commands via Tasking. Safety Services shall monitor efficiency telemetry for anomalies (e.g., sudden energy spike) as a safety indicator. This avoids dual calculation and ensures clear ownership.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (distributed energy monitoring and optimization across Vehicle and Teleoperation) directly addresses the refined FR (enable energy efficiency achievement via measurement, feedback, and control optimization). The DP specifies how to measure energy_per_ton, provide real-time feedback, and support operator-driven optimization. The mapping is clear and non-circular. DP-C supports both on-site and tele-operated modes, aligning with stakeholder needs (Operator, Tele-operator, Site Manager).

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk 1: Energy measurement hardware and firmware are contained in Vehicle (high containment, low diffusion). However, efficiency calculation logic is split between Vehicle (real-time KPI) and Teleoperation (optimization strategy). This is acceptable if interfaces are well-defined.

Diffusion Risk 2: Telemetry interface must carry sufficient data (raw energy, tonnage, KPI) to avoid forcing Teleoperation to re-implement calculation logic. Mitigation: Specify Telemetry schema in detail.

Diffusion Risk 3: Tasking interface must support efficiency-aware commands (e.g., eco-mode, speed profiles). If Tasking is too generic, Teleoperation cannot effectively optimize. Mitigation: Extend Tasking interface with efficiency-aware parameters.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Vehicle containment score is 0.95 (excellent). Teleoperation containment score is 0.60 (moderate) because it owns optimization strategy but depends on Vehicle for measurement. This is a reasonable trade-off: Vehicle is the natural owner of hardware-level measurement; Teleoperation is the natural owner of remote control strategy. The split is justified by domain expertise and architectural clarity.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: (1) Measure energy_per_ton via Vehicle telemetry over a defined evaluation period. (2) Compare measured value to E_target. (3) Conduct controlled trials with both on-site and tele-operated modes. (4) Analyze operator feedback and efficiency logs to assess optimization effectiveness. (5) Audit Telemetry data for consistency and accuracy. Verification requires integration of Vehicle and Teleoperation modules and access to worksite data.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Vehicle owns measurement and real-time KPI calculation. Teleoperation owns feedback presentation and optimization strategy. Safety Services monitors efficiency as a safety indicator. NGHE Solution integrates the capability. Ownership boundaries are explicit and non-overlapping.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Specify the energy_per_ton calculation formula in detail (e.g., energy_per_ton = cumulative_energy_kWh / cumulative_tonnage_moved). Assign calculation ownership (recommend Vehicle for real-time, Teleoperation for post-analysis).
Revision 2: Define Telemetry data schema to include raw energy, tonnage, and KPI values. Specify data rate (≥1 Hz) and latency (<500 ms) requirements for real-time feedback.

Revision 3: Extend Tasking interface to support efficiency-aware control commands (e.g., eco-mode flag, speed profile, load distribution hints). Define command semantics and Vehicle response behavior.

Revision 4: Specify how Vehicle and Teleoperation coordinate efficiency optimization. For example, Vehicle may implement basic eco-mode; Teleoperation may issue advanced control commands based on efficiency feedback.
Revision 5: Define acceptance criteria for energy efficiency KPI. Specify E_target value, evaluation period (e.g., 40-hour work week), and acceptable variance (e.g., ±5%).

decision.justification: The refined FR (CR-NGHE-003 Refined) is not yet atomic and has been decomposed into five sub-FRs (CR-NGHE-003.1 through CR-NGHE-003.5). Each sub-FR addresses a specific aspect of energy efficiency (measurement, feedback, control, logging). The selected DP (DP-C) and assigned IM (Vehicle with Teleoperation support) are sound and pass the gate review. However, to ensure complete functional decomposition and clear allocation of responsibility, each sub-FR must be decomposed to the next level. This will clarify the detailed design of measurement hardware, calculation logic, feedback UI, and logging mechanisms.
decision.justification_detail: The current level (CR-NGHE-003) is a capability-level FR that spans multiple architectural layers (hardware measurement, firmware calculation, software optimization, UI feedback). Decomposing to the next level will allocate each sub-FR to specific modules and design parameters, enabling detailed design and implementation planning. For example, CR-NGHE-003.1 (energy measurement) will decompose into hardware sensor selection, firmware data acquisition, and calibration procedures. CR-NGHE-003.3 (feedback) will decompose into Telemetry message format, UI design, and alert thresholds.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and log energy consumption (kWh or equivalent) from all propulsion and auxiliary systems. [Decompose to: sensor selection, data acquisition, calibration, logging]

CR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and log tonnage moved (mass × distance or equivalent work metric). [Decompose to: load cell selection, GPS/odometry integration, data fusion, logging]

CR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time energy efficiency feedback (energy_per_ton) to operators and tele-operators via Telemetry interface. [Decompose to: KPI calculation, Telemetry message format, UI presentation, alert thresholds]

CR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall support operator/tele-operator control strategies that minimize energy consumption while meeting worksite tasking requirements. [Decompose to: eco-mode logic, speed profile optimization, load distribution hints, Tasking command semantics]

CR-NGHE-003.5: The NGHE solution shall log and report energy efficiency KPI data for post-evaluation analysis and continuous improvement. [Decompose to: data logging format, storage, retrieval, reporting UI, analytics]

assumptions: Assumption 1: Vehicle power measurement systems are accurate and calibrated to ±5% or better.
Assumption 2: Tonnage measurement (via load cells, GPS, or equivalent) is available and integrated into Vehicle telemetry.
Assumption 3: Tele-operators have sufficient training and situational awareness to interpret efficiency feedback and adjust control strategies.
Assumption 4: Telemetry interface can support ≥1 Hz data rate and <500 ms latency for real-time feedback in tele-operation mode.
Assumption 5: Energy efficiency optimization does not conflict with safety or operational constraints (e.g., speed limits, load limits).
Assumption 6: Customer-defined E_target is achievable with the selected Vehicle and Teleoperation design.
open_risks: Risk 1: If Vehicle and Teleoperation use different efficiency calculation methods, KPI values may diverge, causing confusion and audit failures. Mitigation: Define a single, canonical energy_per_ton formula and assign calculation ownership.

Risk 2: Real-time efficiency feedback may overwhelm operators if not presented clearly; poor UI/UX could negate optimization benefits. Mitigation: Conduct user research and iterative UI design with operators and tele-operators.

Risk 3: Network latency in Teleoperation mode may delay efficiency feedback, reducing tele-operator responsiveness to efficiency opportunities. Mitigation: Specify Telemetry latency requirements and test with representative network conditions.

Risk 4: Vehicle firmware may become too complex if it includes measurement, calculation, and optimization logic. Mitigation: Keep Vehicle firmware focused on measurement and basic calculation; delegate advanced optimization to Teleoperation.

Risk 5: Energy efficiency optimization may increase operational complexity and training burden on operators and tele-operators. Mitigation: Provide clear documentation, training, and decision support tools.

Risk 6: Safety criticality (high) requires that energy efficiency optimization does not compromise safety. Mitigation: Integrate with Safety Services; ensure efficiency optimization respects safety constraints.
evidence_needed: Evidence 1: Specification of energy_per_ton calculation formula and ownership (Vehicle vs. Teleoperation).
Evidence 2: Telemetry interface schema (data fields, data types, rate, latency) for energy efficiency KPI.
Evidence 3: Tasking interface extension for efficiency-aware control commands (eco-mode, speed profile, load distribution).
Evidence 4: Vehicle power measurement hardware specifications (sensor type, accuracy, calibration procedure).
Evidence 5: Vehicle tonnage measurement integration (load cells, GPS, data fusion method).
Evidence 6: Teleoperation UI design for efficiency feedback presentation (mockups, user research results).
Evidence 7: Acceptance criteria for energy efficiency KPI (E_target value, evaluation period, acceptable variance).
Evidence 8: Safety analysis of energy efficiency optimization (interaction with safety constraints, risk mitigation).
Evidence 9: Network latency and bandwidth analysis for Teleoperation mode (real-time feedback feasibility).
Evidence 10: Operator and tele-operator training plan for efficiency optimization.
